threats.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Implementation Upgrades Can Brick Wallets or Allow Takeover
threats.hypothesis_description: The `canSkipChainIdValidation` function whitelists `UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector`, allowing implementation upgrades to be replayed across any chain where the wallet exists. If the target implementation address on a destination chain contains malicious code or is empty, replaying the upgrade UserOp will compromise or permanently brick the wallet on that chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cross-chain replayability is a core feature, but allowing implementation upgrades to be replayed assumes the new implementation address is safe and identical across all chains. An attacker can exploit this by deploying malicious code to the same address on a different chain and replaying the user's legitimate upgrade transaction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector is explicitly whitelisted for chain-agnostic execution

preconditions: Wallet owner signs a UserOp to upgrade the implementation using the REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY
Attacker observes the valid UserOp on the source chain
Target implementation address on the destination chain contains different bytecode or no code

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Whitelisting critical state-altering functions for cross-chain replay
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Upgrades change the fundamental logic of the proxy, which is highly chain-dependent
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to verify the bytecode hash of the target implementation on the destination chain

analysis_questions: Can an attacker deterministically deploy malicious code to the target implementation address on other chains?
What happens to the proxy if the target address has no code on the destination chain?
validation_requirements: Simulate an upgrade UserOp on Chain A
Deploy a different contract to the target address on Chain B
Replay the UserOp on Chain B and verify proxy takeover

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User upgrades wallet to Implementation V2 on Base. Attacker deploys a malicious contract at the V2 address on Optimism (e.g., via a different factory or EOA nonce). Attacker replays the Base UserOp on Optimism, upgrading the Optimism wallet to the malicious contract and draining its funds.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy code to a specific address on the destination chain
User initiates a legitimate cross-chain upgrade

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all assets held by the wallet on the destination chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user who upgrades their wallet implementation using the replayable nonce
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Impossible if upgraded to a malicious contract that removes upgradeability

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing cross-chain integration tests verifying implementation bytecode parity during replayed upgrades

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal (gas costs for deployment and UserOp submission)
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed anytime after the source chain UserOp is broadcast
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as an upgradeToAndCall execution

threats.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Owner Removal Causes Unintended Lockouts Due to Index Divergence
threats.hypothesis_description: The `removeOwnerAtIndex` function is whitelisted for cross-chain replay. Because owner indices are assigned sequentially based on local chain state (`nextOwnerIndex`), wallets with divergent owner histories across chains will have different owners at the same index. Replaying a removal UserOp from one chain to another can inadvertently remove a legitimate, active owner on the destination chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: State synchronization across chains is not guaranteed. If a user adds an owner on Chain A but not Chain B, the indices diverge. A subsequent removal by index on Chain A, if replayed on Chain B, will target the wrong owner.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeOwnerAtIndex.selector is whitelisted for execution without chain ID validation

preconditions: Wallet owner state has diverged between two chains (different owners at the same index)
Owner signs a UserOp to remove an owner by index using the REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Deletion by index combined with cross-chain replayability
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Indices are local to the chain's state history, not globally unique identifiers for the owner bytes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No verification that the owner bytes being removed on the destination chain match the intended owner bytes from the source chain

analysis_questions: How easily can owner indices diverge across chains in normal usage?
Can an attacker intentionally cause divergence to set up a targeted removal?
validation_requirements: Create divergent owner states on two simulated chains
Execute removeOwnerAtIndex on Chain A
Replay on Chain B and verify the wrong owner is removed

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User has Owners [Alice, Bob] on Base and [Alice, Charlie] on Optimism. User removes Bob on Base (index 1). Attacker replays this UserOp on Optimism, which removes Charlie (index 1) without the user's consent, potentially locking the user out of the Optimism wallet.
prerequisites: User executes a replayable owner removal

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of access to funds if the removed owner was the only accessible key on that chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users managing multi-chain wallets who do not perfectly sync every owner addition
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible only if another active owner remains on the destination chain

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for cross-chain replay of owner management with divergent state histories

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as an unexpected RemoveOwner event on the destination chain

threats.title: MagicSpend Paymaster Violates ERC-7562 Storage Access Rules, Causing Bundler Rejection
threats.hypothesis_description: The `validatePaymasterUserOp` function checks `address(this).balance` to ensure sufficient funds. However, ERC-7562 strictly prohibits accessing the paymaster's own balance during the validation phase to prevent unpredictable simulation results. Standard ERC-4337 bundlers will automatically reject any UserOp utilizing this paymaster, causing a complete denial of service on public mempools.
threats.investigation_rationale: ERC-4337 bundlers enforce strict storage access rules during the validation phase to prevent DoS attacks against the mempool. Accessing `address(this).balance` is explicitly banned because the balance can change outside of the UserOp's control (e.g., via direct ETH transfers), invalidating the simulation.
preconditions: User attempts to submit a UserOp sponsored by MagicSpend to a standard ERC-4337 public mempool bundler

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing forbidden environmental state (balance) during validation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Bundlers use heavily restricted RPC endpoints (e.g., eth_estimateUserOperationGas) that enforce ERC-7562 rules
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative mechanism to track available liquidity without accessing the raw balance

analysis_questions: Will standard bundlers (e.g., Alchemy, Pimlico) reject this UserOp outright?
Is the protocol relying on a private, modified bundler that bypasses these rules?
validation_requirements: Submit a UserOp using MagicSpend to a compliant ERC-4337 bundler node
Observe the rejection reason (should be a storage access violation)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users attempt to use the MagicSpend paymaster to sponsor their transactions. The bundler simulates `validatePaymasterUserOp`, detects the `EXTBALANCE` or `BALANCE` opcode access on the paymaster's address, and drops the UserOp. The paymaster is completely non-functional for standard users.
prerequisites: Use of standard ERC-4337 bundler infrastructure

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but core protocol functionality is broken
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting to use the paymaster
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires upgrading the paymaster contract to remove the balance check

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests with an actual ERC-7562 compliant bundler (tests likely use mock EntryPoint or bypass simulation rules)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Off-chain bundler logs will show validation rule violations

threats.title: Multiple UserOps in Same Bundle Can Bypass MagicSpend Balance Check
threats.hypothesis_description: In `validatePaymasterUserOp`, the contract checks if its balance can cover `withdrawAmount` for each UserOp independently. If a user submits multiple UserOps in the same bundle, they can collectively exceed the contract's balance because `_withdrawableETH` is credited without reducing the checked balance. This causes `postOp` to fail during execution, wasting bundler gas and risking paymaster penalization.
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation phase runs for all UserOps in a bundle before any execution phase begins. Because the balance check does not account for pending withdrawals from other UserOps in the same bundle, the contract can over-commit its ETH balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Balance check against individual withdrawAmount followed by additive state update to _withdrawableETH

preconditions: MagicSpend contract has a finite ETH balance (e.g., 10 ETH)
User obtains multiple valid WithdrawRequests whose sum exceeds the contract balance
User submits multiple UserOps in the same bundle using these requests

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Independent validation checks for cumulative state constraints
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The second UserOp's postOp will fail because the first UserOp drained the balance, causing an assert failure in postOpReverted mode
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cumulative tracking of pending withdrawals during the validation phase

analysis_questions: Does the EntryPoint penalize the paymaster if postOp reverts in postOpReverted mode?
Can this be used to intentionally grief the paymaster's reputation with bundlers?
validation_requirements: Fund paymaster with 10 ETH
Create two UserOps requesting 8 ETH each
Bundle them together and execute via EntryPoint
Verify the second postOp fails and check EntryPoint state

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker obtains valid withdraw requests (or signs them if they are the owner) and submits a bundle of UserOps that over-commits the paymaster's balance. The validations pass, but during execution, the later `postOp` calls fail due to insufficient ETH. The `postOpReverted` fallback also fails due to the `assert`, causing the bundler to lose gas or the paymaster to be penalized.
prerequisites: Ability to submit bundles to the EntryPoint

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Wasted gas for bundlers, potential reputation loss for the paymaster resulting in blacklisting

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for bundled UserOps that collectively exceed the paymaster's ETH balance

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Analyze gas consumption during the failing postOpReverted call
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires atomic bundling of multiple UserOps

threats.title: Unspent Gas Refund is Permanently Lost if postOp ETH Transfer Fails
threats.hypothesis_description: In `postOp`, the unspent gas refund is calculated and sent to the user via `forceSafeTransferETH`. If the transfer fails (e.g., the user's wallet reverts on receive), `postOp` reverts. The EntryPoint then calls `postOp` with `postOpReverted` mode, but MagicSpend asserts `mode != postOpReverted`, causing it to revert again. The gas refund is never credited to the user's state and is permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: The paymaster intends to refund unspent gas to the user. However, the refund calculation and state update occur in the same function as the external ETH transfer. If the transfer fails, the state update is reverted, and the hardcoded assert prevents the fallback `postOpReverted` from rescuing the refund.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) combined with state updates and external calls in the same function

preconditions: User's smart wallet has a receive() function that reverts or consumes more gas than the stipend
UserOp executes successfully, leaving an unspent gas refund

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hard failure (assert) in the error recovery path (postOpReverted)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The gas refund (maxGasCost - actualGasCost) is only calculated in postOp and is lost if postOp cannot complete successfully
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to credit the refund to _withdrawableETH without attempting the transfer

analysis_questions: Does the user's base _withdrawableETH remain intact after the double revert?
Is the gas refund permanently retained by the MagicSpend contract?
validation_requirements: Deploy a receiver contract that reverts on ETH transfer
Execute a sponsored UserOp from this contract
Verify that the gas refund is not added to the contract's _withdrawableETH

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user with a complex smart wallet (or a malicious bundler restricting gas) executes a sponsored transaction. The `postOp` attempts to send the refund but fails due to OOG or a revert in the receiver. The EntryPoint retries with `postOpReverted`, which hits the `assert` and fails. The user loses their gas refund, which remains stuck in the paymaster.
prerequisites: Receiver contract that fails on standard ETH transfer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of gas refunds for affected users, accumulating in the paymaster
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users with non-standard receiver logic or victims of bundler gas griefing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Funds are stuck in the paymaster; only the admin can withdraw them via ownerWithdraw

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for postOp behavior when the user account rejects the ETH transfer

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as UserOperationEvents with success=false or postOp revert logs

threats.title: RIP-7212 Precompile Fallback Can Be Spoofed on Unsupported Chains
threats.hypothesis_description: The `WebAuthn.verify` function attempts to use the RIP-7212 precompile at address `0x100`. It considers the signature valid if the `staticcall` succeeds, returns data (`ret.length > 0`), and decodes to `1`. On chains without the precompile, if an arbitrary contract is deployed at `0x100` that returns a matching payload, an attacker can bypass signature verification entirely and forge passkey authentications.
threats.investigation_rationale: The library assumes that address `0x100` is either empty (returning no data) or implements the RIP-7212 precompile correctly. On EVM chains where `0x100` is a regular address that can be deployed to, an attacker or existing contract could return data that satisfies the validation check, completely breaking passkey security.
deployment_assumptions: Target chain does not natively support the RIP-7212 precompile at 0x100
An arbitrary contract exists or can be deployed at 0x100 on the target chain that returns a payload decoding to 1
threats.config_evidence: Protocol targets multiple chains (Polygon, BNB, Avalanche) which may not have standardized the 0x100 precompile.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall to 0x100 followed by length > 0 check and abi.decode

preconditions: Contract at 0x100 returns data that decodes to uint256(1) when called with the signature payload

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Optimistic precompile usage without chain ID or codehash verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If 0x100 is a generic fallback contract or attacker-controlled on a specific L2, it bypasses the FCL fallback
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check to ensure 0x100 is actually a precompile (e.g., checking extcodesize == 0 or specific chain support)

analysis_questions: What is the state of address 0x100 on all target chains (Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis)?
Can an attacker deploy to 0x100 on any of these chains?
validation_requirements: Scan all target networks for code at 0x100
Analyze the behavior of any found contracts when called with the ABI encoded arguments

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: On an L2 where 0x100 is an unowned address, an attacker uses a vanity address generator or specific deployment trick to place a contract at 0x100 that always returns `abi.encode(uint256(1))`. The attacker then submits forged UserOps for any passkey-owned wallet on that chain. The `staticcall` succeeds, bypasses FCL, and grants the attacker full control.
prerequisites: Ability to control or find a favorable contract at 0x100 on a target chain

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of funds for all passkey-owned wallets on the affected chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All passkey users on the affected chain

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing cross-chain environmental tests verifying the safety of the 0x100 call on all supported networks

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Cost to deploy to 0x100 if possible
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Requires off-chain monitoring of 0x100 state across chains

threats.title: Passkey Authentication Bypasses User Verification (UV), Reducing Security to Single-Factor
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_validateSignature` function in `CoinbaseSmartWallet` hardcodes `requireUV: false` when calling `WebAuthn.verify`. This instructs the verifier to only check for User Presence (UP) and ignore the User Verified (UV) flag. An attacker with physical access to an unlocked device can authorize arbitrary transactions without needing the user's biometric or PIN authentication, reducing security to single-factor.
threats.investigation_rationale: Passkeys are designed to provide strong two-factor authentication (device possession + biometric/PIN). By explicitly disabling the UV check on-chain, the smart contract downgrades the security model, making it vulnerable to physical device access attacks.
preconditions: Attacker gains physical access to the user's unlocked device containing the passkey
The authenticator allows signing with only User Presence (e.g., tapping a YubiKey or screen)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Users expect passkeys to require FaceID/TouchID, but the contract will accept signatures generated without them
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No option for users to enforce UV for their specific wallet

analysis_questions: Do common authenticators (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager) allow generating assertions without UV if requested?
Is this a deliberate UX tradeoff that users are unaware of?
validation_requirements: Generate a WebAuthn assertion with UP=1 and UV=0
Submit it to the smart wallet and verify it is accepted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user leaves their laptop or phone unlocked. An attacker quickly initiates a transaction to drain the wallet and taps the security key or clicks 'Continue' on the prompt. Because the contract does not require UV, the transaction succeeds without asking for the user's fingerprint or PIN.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of funds for the compromised wallet

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests validating that UV=0 assertions are intentionally accepted and documenting the security tradeoff

threats.title: Wallet Can Be Permanently Bricked by Removing All Owners
threats.hypothesis_description: The `removeOwnerAtIndex` function deletes an owner from the wallet but does not enforce a minimum owner count. An owner can accidentally or maliciously remove all owners, including themselves. Once the last owner is removed, the wallet becomes permanently locked, as no one can authorize new transactions, add owners, or upgrade the implementation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Smart contract wallets must maintain at least one valid authorization mechanism to prevent permanent loss of access. The lack of a minimum owner check allows the wallet to enter a dead-end state from which recovery is impossible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Deletion of owner state without checking remaining active owners

preconditions: Wallet has one or more owners
An authorized owner executes removeOwnerAtIndex until zero owners remain

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted deletion leading to dead-end state
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The EntryPoint validation path relies entirely on _validateSignature, which will fail if no owners exist
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `require(ownerCount > 1)` or similar check before deletion

analysis_questions: Is there any backdoor or recovery mechanism if all owners are removed?
Can the EntryPoint bypass the signature check in any edge case?
validation_requirements: Deploy a wallet with one owner
Call removeOwnerAtIndex to remove the sole owner
Attempt to execute any transaction or add an owner; verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user's secondary device is compromised. The attacker, knowing they cannot easily extract the funds due to off-chain monitoring, decides to grief the user by submitting a UserOp that calls `removeOwnerAtIndex` for all registered owners. The transaction succeeds, and the wallet's funds are permanently locked.
prerequisites: Compromised owner key or severe user error

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of all assets in the wallet

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying behavior when the last owner is removed

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a RemoveOwner event leaving the wallet empty
